author: jtraglia
content:
Hi client devs, it appears that we have a mini-debate about readability/testability in the specs. What do you all think about this?
https://github.com/ethereum/consensus-specs/pull/5336#discussion_r3357386755

author: jtraglia
content: Lol I know potuz is going to agree with you <:crazycry:932693133546889216>

author: potuz
content: What's my task read that issue?

author: jtraglia
content: Just the comment thread really

author: potuz
content: Ok but I feel I'll regret this

author: jtraglia
content: <@586161934425128960> I have another example to share. Which of these would you find easier to read *and* implement?

author: potuz
content: Oh God, I am just finding out hat you merged already some weird `is_previous_slot_payload_decision` predicates... the spec should **not** change that often that people even working on it don't know what the hell is going on

author: jtraglia
content: That's a different topic. And didn't come from me. That refactor was helpful though.

author: potuz
content:
Well, if i had read that refactor I would point out how un-software-engineering an unreadable it is to write 
```
is_payload_decision = node.payload_status in [PAYLOAD_STATUS_EMPTY, PAYLOAD_STATUS_FULL]
```
when there are only 3 possible statuses: asserting that something is in a slice, running the slice linearly, when the check used to be an equality is crazy

author: potuz
content: and it is definitely NOT more readable

author: jtraglia
content: This part is definitely more readable:

author: jtraglia
content: That first condition is ugly & confusing.

author: jtraglia
content: This is much clearer:

author: potuz
content:
````
is_payload_decision = node.payload_status in [PAYLOAD_STATUS_EMPTY, PAYLOAD_STATUS_FULL]
````
Is equivalent to
```
is_payload decision = node.payload_status != PAYLOAD_STATUS_PENDING
```

author: potuz
content: which ones uses more time your brain?

author: jtraglia
content: The latter is much better in my opinion. We can change that.

author: jtraglia
content: But that wasn't really the point of that PR to me.

author: jtraglia
content: Technically the former is safer though.

author: potuz
content:
Anyway, regarding the topic above: I completely agree with this statement from <@586161934425128960> 
```
personally, I find this much harder to read and I would instead go in the other direction and adapt should_build_on_full to be closer to what we currently have in should_extend_payload
```
It's a no-brainer that the former way is much more readable. And the new way is not even Python. No Python coder writes like that

author: potuz
content: The whole function is crazy with a very long and descripting comment that distracts from the function itself: the conditions are obvious and just spelling out what they do is exactly my Claude prompt of what NOT to do on comments.

author: jtraglia
content: What is?

author: jtraglia
content: And do you understand my point about it making testing more difficult?

author: jtraglia
content: Would you feel differently if the easier to test code prevented a bug on mainnet?

author: jtraglia
content: Via a test that would not have existed otherwise.

author: potuz
content: Yeah I do understand that tesing each branch becomes more difficult cause you need to make different assertions on to pass in stead of not caring about the last ones since they won't hold. But anyway when you're testing multiple branches, the example that gets to the very last one already gives you a starting point on how to pass all but the one thing you want to change.

author: potuz
content: And I do find this to be a good argument, if the tests are really much easier to make, but I don't really believe this cause you eventually need to produce a case that passes all ifs except the last one

author: jtraglia
content: Not quite. A test which passes all but the last condition or whatever is not sufficient. Say the spec is `A and B and C` and the impl is `A and C`. A test where all variables are true would pass in both. A test where `C` is false would pass but give the impl a false sense of correctness. In order to know that an impl has a crucial check, there must be a test where `B` is false.

author: potuz
content: Yes, that's clear, but the point is that when you have a case that passes all conditions, then you only grab that case and modify only one of the conditions to pass all but one.

author: potuz
content: what you gain from the cascading if-elses is that you can disregard the remaining ones and modify many if you want to be false, cause you are hoping to fail in a particular spot, but since anyway you need to construct the last case passing all, you can just modify that case

author: potuz
content: I feel your pain or the few people that write or us that debug tests when they fail. But ultimately the most important property that the spec should have is to be readable and understandable.

author: potuz
content: The more time I spend and waste reading the markdown the worse the spec is

author: potuz
content: most people don't need to ever look at tests

author: jtraglia
content: I don't fully understand this. Could you provide a simple example? Given `A and B and C`, to check that `B` exists, are you saying  to set `A` and `C` to false? How would that help? The result would be false regardless of `B`'s value.

author: jtraglia
content: But there is a balance and testing is also very important.

author: jtraglia
content: <@586161934425128960> iirc, there were some missing gossip validation checks in lodestar which tests identified, right?

author: nflaig
content: hmm I do find both have some advantages, I do like variable assigments like this

author: nflaig
content: yes, I think the gossip validation tests and p2p-interface refactoring is good, we also had bunch of security reports in the past due to missing or incorrect gossip validation checks, it's not only nice for correctness, but also if a new condition is added in a new release, you don't miss it when you bump the spec
